title: What is AI, ML & Why It Matters Today
learningObjectives: Distinguish AI, ML, Deep Learning, and GenAI clearly
Explain the 4-level hierarchy with one example per level
Name three AI applications in everyday software
Explain why data quality matters for AI outcomes
Describe what narrow AI can and cannot do
Answer foundational AI interview questions confidently

sections.title: Why AI Fundamentals Are Non-Negotiable
sections.content:
Every software engineering interview in 2025 and beyond will involve AI in some form. But 'AI' has become a term so overloaded that engineers use it to mean everything from a basic if-else recommendation to a multi-billion-parameter language model.

The engineers who stand out are not those who say *'AI learns from data'* — everyone says that. They are the ones who can say *'This is a supervised classification problem, here is why we'd use a transformer over a CNN for it, and here is the data quality issue that would kill it in production.'*

Day 1 gives you the conceptual map. Every subsequent day builds on this foundation. Without it, you will use the right words in the wrong contexts — which is worse than not using them at all.

sections.title: AI, ML, DL, GenAI — The Complete Hierarchy
sections.content:
### 1. The Four-Level Hierarchy

Think of these as concentric circles — each inner circle is a subset of the one outside it:

| Level | Definition | Example |
|-------|------------|----------|
| **AI** | Any machine mimicking human intelligence | Chess engine, spam filter, voice assistant |
| **ML** | AI that learns patterns from data | Fraud detection, Netflix recommendations |
| **DL** | ML using multi-layer neural networks | Image recognition, speech-to-text |
| **GenAI** | DL that generates new content | ChatGPT, DALL-E, GitHub Copilot |

**Key insight**: Not all AI is ML. A rule-based chatbot (`if user says 'hello' → respond 'Hi!'`) is AI but not ML.

---

### 2. Historical Milestones

- **1950** — Turing Test proposed
- **1956** — 'Artificial Intelligence' coined at Dartmouth
- **2012** — AlexNet wins ImageNet; deep learning era begins
- **2017** — Transformer paper ('Attention Is All You Need')
- **2020** — GPT-3: first large-scale general-purpose language model
- **2022** — ChatGPT: GenAI becomes mainstream
- **2024–25** — Claude 4, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5: multimodal reasoning at scale

---

### 3. AI in Everyday Software

- **Gmail** — Smart Reply, spam detection (ML classification)
- **YouTube/Netflix** — Recommendation engine (collaborative filtering + DL)
- **Google Maps** — Traffic prediction (temporal ML)
- **Face ID** — Facial recognition (deep CNN)
- **GitHub Copilot** — Code completion (LLM fine-tuned on code)
- **Spotify** — Discover Weekly (audio features + user behavior ML)

---

### 4. Why Data Quality Is Everything

| Data problem | Effect on model |
|---|---|
| Missing values | Model learns to predict missing as a valid class |
| Label errors | Model learns wrong associations accurately |
| Historical bias | Model perpetuates bias at production scale |
| Distribution shift | Training data ≠ real-world data → silent failures |
| Duplicate rows | Model overfits to repeated examples |

**Rule of thumb**: 80% of an ML project's time is data preparation, not modeling.

---

### 5. Narrow AI vs General AI (AGI)

**Narrow AI** (all AI that exists today):
- Excellent at one specific domain
- Cannot transfer skills to other domains
- Examples: AlphaGo (Go only), GPT (text only), Whisper (speech-to-text only)

**AGI** (hypothetical):
- Performs any intellectual task a human can
- Transfers knowledge across domains
- Does not currently exist

LLMs *feel* like AGI because they're trained on so many tasks, but they're still narrow: each session starts fresh with no persistent memory, and they cannot interact with the physical world without tools.

---

### 6. What AI Cannot Do (Production Reality)

- **Real-time awareness** — no live internet without tools
- **Persistent memory** — each session starts fresh
- **100% accuracy** — probabilistic by nature; always has an error rate
- **Causal reasoning** — correlates patterns; doesn't understand cause and effect
- **Physical actions** — cannot interact with the world directly

sections.title: AI Hierarchy Concept Demo
sections.description: Interactive demo showing rule-based AI vs ML with a data quality impact analysis.
sections.code:
# AI Hierarchy Demo — no ML libraries needed

print("=" * 55)
print("LEVEL 1: AI — Rule-Based (no learning)")
print("=" * 55)

def rule_based_assistant(user_input):
    user_input = user_input.lower()
    if 'hello' in user_input or 'hi' in user_input:
        return "Hello! How can I help you?"
    elif 'weather' in user_input:
        return "I don't have weather data."
    elif 'bye' in user_input:
        return "Goodbye!"
    return "I don't understand that."

for msg in ["Hello!", "What's the weather?", "Random question"]:
    print(f"User: {msg}  →  Bot: {rule_based_assistant(msg)}")

print()
print("=" * 55)
print("LEVEL 2: ML — Learns from labeled examples")
print("=" * 55)

training_data = [
    ("great product", "positive"),
    ("terrible service", "negative"),
    ("amazing quality", "positive"),
    ("awful experience", "negative"),
]

positive_words, negative_words = set(), set()
for text, label in training_data:
    words = set(text.split())
    if label == "positive":
        positive_words |= words
    else:
        negative_words |= words

def ml_sentiment(text):
    words = set(text.lower().split())
    pos = len(words & positive_words)
    neg = len(words & negative_words)
    return "positive" if pos > neg else "negative" if neg > pos else "neutral"

for review in ["great service", "terrible quality", "okay product"]:
    print(f"  '{review}' → {ml_sentiment(review)}")

print()
print("=" * 55)
print("DATA QUALITY IMPACT")
print("=" * 55)

dataset = [
    {"text": "excellent product", "label": "positive"},
    {"text": "great value",       "label": "positive"},
    {"text": None,               "label": "positive"},  # missing!
    {"text": "terrible",         "label": "positive"},  # wrong label!
    {"text": "bad quality",      "label": "negative"},
    {"text": "bad quality",      "label": "negative"},  # duplicate!
]

total = len(dataset)
missing = sum(1 for d in dataset if d['text'] is None)
duplicates = 1  # 'bad quality' appears twice
clean = total - missing - duplicates

print(f"Total:      {total} rows")
print(f"Missing:    {missing} rows")
print(f"Duplicates: {duplicates} rows")
print(f"Clean:      {clean} rows — model quality is CAPPED at data quality")
print("\n✅ You now understand the AI hierarchy")
sections.output:
=======================================================
LEVEL 1: AI — Rule-Based (no learning)
=======================================================
User: Hello!             →  Bot: Hello! How can I help you?
User: What's the weather? →  Bot: I don't have weather data.
User: Random question    →  Bot: I don't understand that.

=======================================================
LEVEL 2: ML — Learns from labeled examples
=======================================================
  'great service' → positive
  'terrible quality' → negative
  'okay product' → neutral

=======================================================
DATA QUALITY IMPACT
=======================================================
Total:      6 rows
Missing:    1 rows
Duplicates: 1 rows
Clean:      4 rows — model quality is CAPPED at data quality

✅ You now understand the AI hierarchy

sections.title: Common Misconceptions About AI
items: **'AI understands language'** — LLMs predict statistically likely tokens; they do not understand meaning the way humans do.
**'More data always helps'** — Dirty, biased, or irrelevant data degrades model performance. Quality beats quantity.
**'AI can do anything a human can'** — Current AI is narrow. It excels at pattern matching within its training distribution.
**'Fine-tuning fixes hallucination'** — Fine-tuning changes style and format; hallucination is structural. RAG is the fix for factual accuracy.
**'AI models are deterministic'** — At temperature > 0, outputs are sampled probabilistically. Same input can produce different outputs.

sections.title: Map AI Applications to Their Level
sections.problem:
For each application below, classify it as: Rule-based AI, ML, Deep Learning, or GenAI — and write one sentence of reasoning.

1. Gmail spam filter
2. A thermostat that turns heating on at 18°C
3. GitHub Copilot completing your code
4. Google Photos recognizing your face
5. Spotify recommending a playlist
6. A chess engine using minimax algorithm
7. DALL-E generating an image from a text prompt
hints: Rule-based: uses if/else or lookup tables with no training data
ML: trained on labeled examples; produces a prediction or classification
Deep Learning: uses neural networks with many layers; often for images, audio, or text
GenAI: produces new content (text, images, audio) not copied from training data
sections.solution:
1. Gmail spam filter → ML (trained on labeled spam/not-spam emails)
2. Thermostat → Rule-based AI (explicit threshold, no learning)
3. GitHub Copilot → GenAI (LLM generates new code from context)
4. Google Photos face recognition → Deep Learning (CNN trained on face images)
5. Spotify recommendations → ML (collaborative filtering + audio feature analysis)
6. Chess minimax engine → Rule-based AI (search algorithm, no training data)
7. DALL-E → GenAI (diffusion model generates novel images from text)

sections.title: Interview Questions — AI Fundamentals

conceptual.question: What is the difference between AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning?
conceptual.answer: AI is the broad goal of making machines exhibit intelligent behavior. Machine Learning is a subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data without explicit rules. Deep Learning is a subset of ML using neural networks with many layers. Think of them as concentric circles: all DL is ML; all ML is AI; not all AI is ML.

followUps.question: Where does Generative AI fit in this hierarchy?
followUps.answer: GenAI is a subset of Deep Learning focused on models that generate new content — text, images, audio, or code. GPT-4 is a DL model (transformer) that generates text. DALL-E is a DL model (diffusion) that generates images. GenAI is the newest inner circle of the hierarchy.

conceptual.question: Why does data quality matter more than model complexity in most ML projects?
conceptual.answer: A model trained on dirty data learns dirty patterns accurately. In practice, 80% of ML project time is data cleaning and feature engineering. A simple logistic regression on clean, well-engineered features often beats a transformer on noisy, mislabeled data. Data quality is the ceiling; model choice is an optimization below that ceiling.

seniorScenario.question: Your team is asked to add 'AI features' to a customer support platform. Walk me through how you'd decide what kind of AI to use.
seniorScenario.answer: First, decompose 'AI features' into specific tasks: ticket routing (classification), response drafting (generation), escalation detection (binary classification), knowledge base search (retrieval). Then match each to the right approach: routing → ML classifier on historical tickets; response drafting → LLM with RAG over past successful responses; escalation → binary classifier; search → embedding-based semantic search. Key decisions: data availability (do we have labeled examples?), latency (real-time vs. batch?), cost (LLM per-token vs. self-hosted ML), and error tolerance (wrong routing is cheap; wrong response draft is expensive).

wrongAnswers: **Wrong:** 'AI is just another word for machine learning.' — **Correction:** AI is the broader field. Rule-based systems, expert systems, and search algorithms are AI but not ML.

**Wrong:** 'More training data always makes the model better.' — **Correction:** Data quality matters more than quantity. Noisy, mislabeled, or biased data actively harms performance even in large volumes.

questions.question: Which of the following is AI but NOT Machine Learning?

options.A: A spam filter trained on email examples
options.B: A chatbot with hardcoded if/else rules for responses
options.C: GPT-4 generating text from a prompt
options.D: A face recognition system trained on photos

questions.explanation: A hardcoded rule-based chatbot exhibits intelligent behavior (AI) but learns nothing from data (not ML). All other options involve learning from training data.

questions.question: What distinguishes Generative AI from other ML systems?

options.B: It classifies inputs into predefined categories
options.C: It generates new content not directly copied from training data

questions.explanation: GenAI models generate novel artifacts (text, images, code) rather than predicting a predefined label or score. The content is synthesized, not retrieved.

questions.question: Why does 'data quality > model complexity' in most real ML projects?

options.A: Complex models are slow to train
options.B: A model trained on dirty data accurately learns incorrect patterns
options.D: Data cleaning is faster than model selection

questions.explanation: Models learn patterns faithfully — including errors. Incorrect labels, missing values, and biases are learned as accurately as correct patterns. No model complexity overcomes fundamental data quality issues.

questions.question: AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) refers to:

options.A: Any AI that uses a neural network with many layers
options.B: AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can, across all domains
options.C: The most powerful current LLMs like GPT-4 and Claude
options.D: AI trained on general-purpose internet text

questions.explanation: AGI is hypothetical — human-level performance across all intellectual tasks with cross-domain knowledge transfer. It does not currently exist. LLMs are narrow AI, very capable within their domain but unable to transfer skills outside it.

sections.title: AI Fundamentals — Cheatsheet
sections.content:
### The Hierarchy (concentric circles)
**AI ⊃ ML ⊃ Deep Learning ⊃ GenAI**

| Level | Learns from data? | Generates content? | Example |
|-------|-------------------|--------------------|----------|
| Rule-based AI | No | No | Thermostat, chess minimax |
| ML | Yes | No | Spam filter, recommendations |
| Deep Learning | Yes (neural nets) | No | Face ID, speech-to-text |
| GenAI | Yes (massive scale) | Yes | ChatGPT, DALL-E, Copilot |

### Data Quality Rules
- Missing values → impute or drop before training
- Label errors → model learns the error perfectly
- Duplicates → causes overfitting to repeated examples
- Check: `df.isnull().sum()` and `df.duplicated().sum()`

### Narrow AI vs AGI
- **Narrow AI** = today's reality (all LLMs, image classifiers, recommenders)
- **AGI** = hypothetical; does not exist
- LLMs feel general because they're trained on diverse data, but each session starts fresh

### What AI Cannot Do Today
- Real-time awareness (without external tools)
- Persistent memory across sessions (without external memory systems)
- True causal reasoning (models correlate, not cause-and-effect)
- Physical world interaction without robotics integration
